title: Time Shifting
summary: This page introduces three fundamental signal operations: shifting, scaling, and inversion, focusing specifically on time shifting. It explains that delaying a signal x(t) by T seconds yields x(t-T), with positive T causing a right shift (delay) and negative T causing a left shift (advance). The concept is illustrated with figures showing a signal and its delayed and advanced versions.

figures.caption: Illustration of time-shifting a signal x(t): (a) original signal, (b) delayed signal x(t−T) shifted right by T, and (c) advanced signal x(t+T) shifted left by T.
figures.description: The figure has three stacked time-domain plots with horizontal axis t and a vertical reference at t=0. Panel (a) shows the original signal x(t); panel (b) shows φ(t)=x(t−T), the same waveform shifted to the right by T (delay); panel (c) shows x(t+T), the same waveform shifted to the left by T (advance). Dotted vertical lines and arrows labeled T mark the horizontal displacement, and students should notice that only the timing changes while the signal shape and amplitude stay the same.
